metadata.title: Why wrapping the same text in XML changes the answer Claude gives
metadata.topic: Input structure as a retrieval constraint
metadata.purpose: Trained on consistently formatted data, the model builds strong statistical expectations for those shapes; inputs matching the training distribution are processed more coherently, outliers less so.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.exclusions: XML schema specifics, other markup formats, tokenization, attention internals
metadata.visual_object: A before/after pane — unstructured text left, tagged XML right — with output arrows diverging.
metadata.hook: The identical product description yields a mediocre summary as plain text but a sharp one wrapped in XML tags — same content, different result.

beats.narration_text: `Product Name: LeapFrog... Letters and words woven...` vs. the same inside `<search_result><title>LeapFrog</title><content>...</content></search_result>` produces noticeably different synthesis.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: The content is identical, so the answer should be too — here structure alone shifts quality; why does formatting act as a constraint the model won't relax?
The identical product description yields a mediocre summary as plain text but a sharp one wrapped in XML tags — same content, different result.
Trained on consistently formatted data, the model builds strong statistical expectations for those shapes; inputs matching the training distribution are processed more coherently, outliers less so.
beats.visual: A before/after pane — unstructured text left, tagged XML right — with output arrows diverging.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: `Product Name: Robot Building Kit. 200 pieces.` vs. `<item><title>Robot Building Kit</title><content>200 pieces</content><category>STEM</category></item>`.
Trained on consistently formatted data, the model builds strong statistical expectations for those shapes; inputs matching the training distribution are processed more coherently, outliers less so.
